content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 31 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 116v–116r | **Source Images:** DEU_031_page_001.jpg, DEU_031_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 162–163 | **Images:** DEU_031_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_031_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Moses went and spoke these words to all Israel.

**2** And he said to them, "I am a hundred and twenty years old today. I am no longer able to go out and come in. The LORD has said to me, 'You shall not cross over this Jordan.'

**3** The LORD your God himself will cross over before you. He will destroy these nations before you, and you shall dispossess them. Joshua will cross over before you, as the LORD has spoken.

**4** And the LORD will do to them as he did to Sihon and Og, the kings of the Amorites, and to their land, when he destroyed them.

**5** And the LORD will give them over to you, and you shall do to them according to the whole commandment that I have commanded you.

**6** Be strong and courageous [chizqu ve-imtsu]. Do not be afraid or be in dread of them, for it is the LORD your God who goes with you. He will not leave you or forsake you."

**7** Then Moses summoned Joshua and said to him in the sight of all Israel, "Be strong and courageous [chazaq ve-ematz], for you shall go with this people into the land that the LORD has sworn to their fathers to give them, and you shall put them in possession of it.

**8** It is the LORD who goes before you. He will be with you; he will not leave you or forsake you. Do not fear or be dismayed."

**9** Then Moses wrote this instruction [torah] and gave it to the priests, the sons of Levi, who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and to all the elders of Israel.

**10** And Moses commanded them: "At the end of every seven years, at the set time in the year of release [shemittah], at the Feast of Booths [Sukkot],

**11** when all Israel comes to appear before the LORD your God at the place that he will choose, you shall read this instruction [torah] before all Israel in their hearing.

**12** Assemble the people — men, women, and little ones, and the sojourner within your towns — that they may hear and learn to fear the LORD your God, and be careful to do all the words of this instruction [torah],

**13** and that their children, who have not known it, may hear and learn to fear the LORD your God, as long as you live in the land that you are crossing the Jordan to possess."

**14** And the LORD said to Moses, "Behold, the days approach when you must die. Call Joshua and present yourselves in the tent of meeting, that I may commission him." So Moses and Joshua went and presented themselves in the tent of meeting.

**15** And the LORD appeared in the tent in a pillar of cloud, and the pillar of cloud stood over the entrance of the tent.

**16** And the LORD said to Moses, "Behold, you are about to lie down with your fathers. Then this people will rise and whore after the foreign gods among them in the land that they are entering, and they will forsake me and break my covenant that I have made with them.

**17** Then my anger will be kindled against them in that day, and I will forsake them and hide my face from them, and they will be devoured. And many evils and troubles will come upon them, so that they will say in that day, 'Have not these evils come upon us because our God is not among us?'

**18** And I will surely hide my face in that day on account of all the evil that they have done, for they have turned to other gods.

**19** Now therefore write this song and teach it to the sons of Israel. Put it in their mouths, that this song may be a witness for me against the sons of Israel.

**20** For when I have brought them into the land flowing with milk and honey, which I swore to give to their fathers, and they have eaten and are full and grown fat, they will turn to other gods and serve them, and despise me and break my covenant.

**21** And when many evils and troubles have come upon them, this song shall confront them as a witness, for it will not be forgotten from the mouth of their offspring. For I know what they are inclined to do even today, before I have brought them into the land that I swore to give."

**22** So Moses wrote this song the same day and taught it to the sons of Israel.

**23** And the LORD commissioned Joshua the son of Nun and said, "Be strong and courageous [chazaq ve-ematz], for you shall bring the sons of Israel into the land that I swore to give them. I will be with you."

**24** When Moses had finished writing the words of this instruction [torah] in a book to the very end,

**25** Moses commanded the Levites who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD:

**26** "Take this Book of the Instruction [sefer ha-torah] and put it by the side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, that it may be there for a witness against you.

**27** For I know your rebellion and your stubbornness. Behold, even today while I am still alive among you, you have been rebellious against the LORD. How much more after my death!

**28** Assemble to me all the elders of your tribes and your officers, that I may speak these words in their hearing and call heaven and earth to witness against them.

**29** For I know that after my death you will surely act corruptly and turn aside from the way that I have commanded you. And evil will befall you in the latter days, because you will do what is evil in the sight of the LORD, provoking him to anger through the work of your hands."

**30** Then Moses spoke the words of this song until they were finished, in the hearing of all the assembly of Israel:

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 31**

**"I am no longer able to go out and come in" (v. 2):** The idiom *lo-ukhal od latset vavo* refers to active leadership capacity, not merely physical mobility. "Going out and coming in" is standard biblical idiom for leading the people in military and civic affairs (cf. Num 27:17; 1 Kgs 3:7). Moses is not saying he cannot walk; he is saying his capacity for leadership at the head of the nation is ended. The LXX renders *dynamatos exienai kai eisporeuomai* (δυνατὸς ἐξιέναι καὶ εἰσπορεύεσθαι), "able to go out and go in," closely following the Hebrew. The NIV's "go out and come in" is also accepted; LOGOS retains this precisely.

**Chizqu ve-imtsu (vv. 6, 7, 23):** The double imperative — "be strong and courageous" — is the signature command of the commissioning formula in Deuteronomy and Joshua. *Chazaq* (חֲזַק) from the root meaning "be strong/firm"; *amats* (אָמַץ) meaning "be resolute/courageous." The LXX renders *andrizou kai ischue* (ἀνδρίζου καὶ ἴσχυε), "act manfully and be strong," at v. 6, and similarly throughout. LOGOS preserves both imperatives rather than collapsing them into one ("be strong" alone). The formula appears first to all Israel (v. 6), then to Joshua before all Israel (v. 7), and finally from the LORD directly to Joshua (v. 23) — a threefold ascending commissioning.

**Hakhel — the assembly (vv. 10–13):** The Hakhel ceremony — a public reading of the Torah every seven years at Sukkot, in the sabbatical year (shemittah) — is a distinctive institution found only here. The word *hakhel* (הַקְהֵל, "assemble!") gave the ceremony its rabbinic name. The inclusion of "men, women, and little ones, and the sojourner" (v. 12) is remarkable: the entire community, including non-Israelites residing among them, is to hear the Torah read. The LXX has *ekklesiason ton laon* (ἐκκλησίασον τὸν λαόν), "assemble the people," which is reflected in the Greek *ekklesia* ("assembly/church"), a significant term in later Christian usage.

**"The Book of the Instruction" — sefer ha-torah (v. 26):** This phrase is of critical importance for the history of the canon. The *sefer ha-torah* is to be deposited beside (*mitsad*) the ark of the covenant as a witness. The LXX has *to biblion tou nomou toutou* (τὸ βιβλίον τοῦ νόμου τούτου), "this book of the law." The placement "by the side of the ark" (not inside it — the tablets were inside) is a significant detail preserved by both Hebrew and Greek traditions.

**"Hide my face" — haster astir panai (v. 18):** The doubled infinitive absolute *haster astir* is emphatic — "I will surely hide my face." The hiding of the divine face (*hester panim*) is a profound theological concept: divine absence in response to covenant violation. The LXX renders *apostrepsas apokrypto* (ἀποστρέφων ἀποκρύψω), "turning away I will hide," which captures the intensification but loses the dramatic Hebrew doubling.

**"That this song may be a witness" (v. 19):** The *shirah* — the Song of Moses that follows in chapter 32 — is here explicitly identified as a legal *witness* (*ed*) against Israel, not merely a poem or hymn. This is a remarkable genre designation: the song functions as a covenantal treaty-document witness. The LXX has *eis martyrion* (εἰς μαρτύριον), "for a witness," matching the Hebrew precisely.

**Verse 1 — LXX divergence:** The LXX of v. 1 reads "And Moses finished speaking all these words to all Israel" (*kai sunetelesen Moyses lalon pantas tous logous toutous*), adding "finished" (*sunetelesen*) and indicating a completion. The MT (Leningrad Codex) reads simply *vayelech Moshe vayedabber* — "And Moses went and spoke." This is one of the notable MT/LXX divergences at the opening of the chapter; I follow the MT.

— LOGOS
